players.name: Gregor Kobel
players.bio: Dortmund's commanding shot-stopper, Kobel is Switzerland's first choice between the sticks. Athletic and commanding in his area, he's been among the Bundesliga's top keepers. His 21 caps belie his quality.
players.facts: Kobel kept a clean sheet on his senior Swiss debut — a solid start for a man whose job is literally to keep things out.

players.name: Miro Muheim
players.bio: Left-back Muheim has risen through German football's second tier with Hamburg, bringing energy and attacking intent down the flank. Still building his international career with 10 caps, this World Cup could be his moment.
players.facts: Muheim plays for Hamburg — a club so big they spent 12 years outside the Bundesliga. Relatable underdog energy.

players.name: Silvan Widmer
players.bio: A veteran of 60 caps with five international goals, Widmer is Nati's reliable right-back with a knack for arriving late into dangerous positions. A Mainz stalwart and a seasoned campaigner heading to his second World Cup.
players.facts: Widmer has scored 5 goals as a right-back for Switzerland — more than several Swiss strikers. Defenders, take note.

players.name: Nico Elvedi
players.bio: Composed, intelligent and technically assured, Elvedi has been a cornerstone of the Swiss backline for nearly a decade. With 67 caps, the Gladbach centre-back brings calm authority to a defence that rarely panics.
players.facts: Elvedi has played over 60 times for Switzerland without ever being remembered for the wrong reasons. Peak defender behaviour.

players.name: Manuel Akanji
players.bio: One of Europe's most complete centre-backs, Akanji won the Champions League with Man City in 2023 before moving to Inter Milan. Powerful, composed and excellent in possession, he's Switzerland's defensive cornerstone at 81 caps.
players.facts: Akanji won the Champions League, Premier League AND FA Cup treble with Man City in 2023. Not bad for a lad from Wiesendangen.

players.name: Denis Zakaria
players.bio: A powerful, box-to-box midfielder with an eye for goal, Zakaria has plied his trade across Europe's top leagues. With 65 caps and experience at Juventus and Chelsea, the Monaco midfielder adds steel and drive to Swiss midfield.
players.facts: Zakaria has played in Switzerland, Germany, Italy, England and France. He's basically done a Grand Tour of European football.

players.name: Breel Embolo
players.bio: Born in Yaoundé, raised in Switzerland, Embolo chose the Nati and never looked back. A powerful, direct striker with 24 international goals, he famously scored against Cameroon — the nation of his birth — at World Cup 2022.
players.facts: Embolo scored vs Cameroon at WC2022 and didn't celebrate out of respect. Awkward? Yes. Classy? Absolutely yes.

players.name: Remo Freuler
players.bio: The engine of Swiss midfield for over a decade, Freuler's relentless pressing and intelligent distribution have made him indispensable. Now 34, the Bologna man heads into his third World Cup — a true Nati warrior with 88 caps.
players.facts: Freuler is from Ennenda — a village so small you've never heard of it. He responded by playing 88 times for Switzerland.

players.name: Johan Manzambi
players.bio: Born in 2005, Manzambi is one of football's most exciting teenagers. The SC Freiburg midfielder has already netted three times in just 12 caps, announcing himself as Switzerland's most thrilling young talent heading into 2026.
players.facts: Manzambi was born in 2005 — the year Switzerland hosted the Confederations Cup. He's basically a Swiss football souvenir.

players.name: Granit Xhaka
players.bio: Switzerland's all-time most-capped player with 146 appearances, Xhaka is a warrior, leader and occasionally a lightning rod. The former Arsenal fan favourite now captains the Nati, marshalling midfield with the authority of a man who's seen it all.
players.facts: Xhaka has been booked so many times he practically has his own loyalty card at FIFA's disciplinary committee.

players.name: Dan Ndoye
players.bio: Quick, direct and full of tricks, Ndoye terrorises full-backs with his pace and directness. The Nottingham Forest winger has eight goals in 31 caps and is rapidly emerging as one of Europe's most dangerous wide players.
players.facts: Ndoye was born in Nyon — home of UEFA headquarters. Growing up next to football's governing body clearly worked out.

players.name: Yvon Mvogo
players.bio: Cameroon-born Mvogo has been a dependable deputy for the Nati, collecting 13 caps across a career spanning multiple European clubs. Now at Lorient, the goalkeeper brings experience and composure as Kobel's capable backup.
players.facts: Like teammate Embolo, Mvogo was born in Yaoundé, Cameroon, but represents Switzerland. The Nati's Cameroonian pipeline is real.

players.name: Ricardo Rodriguez
players.bio: With 138 caps, Rodriguez is a Swiss legend — a serene, elegant left-back whose reading of the game has compensated for any loss of pace over the years. Now at Real Betis, he's one of Switzerland's most experienced players ever.
players.facts: Ricardo Rodriguez has 138 caps. He's appeared for Switzerland more times than most players have had hot dinners.

players.name: Ardon Jashari
players.bio: Jashari is the hottest property in Swiss football — a technically gifted, combative midfielder who earned a big move to AC Milan at just 22. With only 8 caps, this World Cup could catapult him onto the world stage.
players.facts: Jashari joined AC Milan before turning 23 — a club with 18 league titles. No pressure, Ardon, no pressure at all.

players.name: Djibril Sow
players.bio: A disciplined, hard-working midfielder who reads the game exceptionally well, Sow has been a trusted option for Switzerland across 52 caps. Currently plying his trade at Sevilla, he's the kind of player coaches adore.
players.facts: Sow has 52 caps and zero international goals — which for a central midfielder is practically a badge of professional honour.

players.name: Christian Fassnacht
players.bio: A clever, technical attacking midfielder who has been a key figure at Young Boys, Fassnacht uses intelligence over pace to devastating effect. His five international goals in 23 caps show he contributes when called upon.
players.facts: Fassnacht still plays for Young Boys — Switzerland's most successful recent club. Local hero in the truest sense.

players.name: Rubén Vargas
players.bio: Vargas is a pacey, creative left winger who has earned 61 caps and 11 goals with his direct, unpredictable style. Born in Adligenswil, the Sevilla man is one of Switzerland's most consistent wide threats.
players.facts: Vargas has represented Switzerland at senior level since 2019 and has never scored an ugly goal. Strictly aesthetics only.

players.name: Eray Cömert
players.bio: Cömert is a dependable, technically sound centre-back who has navigated a challenging spell at Valencia with professionalism. With 22 caps, he provides solid depth in Switzerland's defensive options at this World Cup.
players.facts: Cömert plays for Valencia — a club in seemingly permanent turmoil. Staying calm at the back is practically a survival skill.

players.name: Noah Okafor
players.bio: Okafor is a lightning-quick, versatile attacker who can play across the front line. After spells at RB Salzburg and AC Milan, he's now at Leeds United, carrying his Swiss pedigree and looking to make his mark at a first World Cup.
players.facts: Okafor scored in the Champions League for Salzburg and played for Milan — Leeds United is quite the career plot twist.

players.name: Michel Aebischer
players.bio: Aebischer is a versatile, intelligent midfielder who can operate in multiple roles across the middle. His 40 caps and consistent performances at Bologna — now Pisa — reflect a player of quiet but undeniable quality.
players.facts: Aebischer is from Fribourg, a bilingual Swiss city. He speaks French and German, making team meetings only half-confusing.

players.name: Marvin Keller
players.bio: Swiss-born in London, Keller is the third-choice keeper with just one cap to his name. The Young Boys goalkeeper is one for the future — but earning a World Cup squad place at 23 is an achievement in itself.
players.facts: Keller was born in London but plays for Switzerland. He's proof that passports and postcodes don't always match footballers.

players.name: Fabian Rieder
players.bio: Rieder is a technically gifted attacking midfielder with a bright future. The Augsburg man has 28 caps and has shown flashes of real quality for the Nati. This World Cup could be the tournament where he announces himself globally.
players.facts: Rieder was born in Bern — Switzerland's capital. Playing for your country is almost a civic duty when you're from the capital.

players.name: Zeki Amdouni
players.bio: Amdouni is a sharp, clever striker with an outstanding conversion rate — 11 goals in just 29 caps is elite efficiency. The Burnley forward has the technical quality and finishing touch to cause any defence serious problems.
players.facts: Amdouni has 11 goals in 29 caps — a ratio that would make many a centre-forward weep into their protein shake.

players.name: Aurèle Amenda
players.bio: At just 22, Amenda is one of Switzerland's most promising defenders, earning his big move to Eintracht Frankfurt after impressing with his composure and aerial ability. Seven caps in and the future looks very bright indeed.
players.facts: Amenda is from Biel/Bienne — Switzerland's most bilingual city. He's comfortable in tight spaces on and off the pitch.

players.name: Luca Jaquez
players.bio: Jaquez is a young centre-back with real potential, earning a move to VfB Stuttgart and three senior caps. Still learning at international level, this World Cup represents a remarkable opportunity for the 23-year-old Luzern native.
players.facts: Jaquez has 3 caps and plays for Stuttgart — a Champions League club. From Luzern to European football in record time.

players.name: Cedric Itten
players.bio: A physical, hard-working striker who leads the line with passion, Itten has five international goals across 15 caps. The Fortuna Düsseldorf forward is Switzerland's reliable backup option up top with a knack for the big moment.
players.facts: Itten once played for Rangers in Scotland — making him one of very few Swiss players to have experienced an Old Firm derby atmosphere.
